title: Rasin Application Security and Reliability Audit
executiveSummary: The active application in this repository is an Express server plus static frontend assets using Supabase Auth and a proxied Supabase gateway. It is not a Next.js application.

The highest-risk findings are committed secrets, over-broad authorization, service_role-backed storage actions reachable from user-controlled flows, an unsafe signup path, and multiple stored XSS paths.
This report is persisted in admin so findings can be tracked and converted into tickets over time.
topRisks: Committed live secrets and privileged Supabase credentials
Moderators are granted broad admin access
Service-role storage operations are reachable from user-controlled flows
Any tree member can email the entire tree
Public signup endpoint creates email-confirmed users and logs credentials
Stored XSS through unsanitized innerHTML rendering
Invite and discovery actions are too broadly available
Intended security middleware is not active
Admin list endpoints do full-table reads and filter in memory
No Supabase RLS or storage policy definitions are present in this repo

findings.title: Committed live secrets and privileged Supabase credentials
findings.issue: The repository contains a committed .env with live-looking database credentials, Supabase service_role, VAPID private key, Resend API key, and webhook secret.
findings.whyItMatters: Anyone with repository access can impersonate the backend, bypass storage and database protections via service_role, send email as the application, and forge webhook requests.
evidence: .env contains DATABASE_URL, SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY, VAPID_PRIVATE_KEY, RESEND_API_KEY, and SEND_EMAIL_HOOK_SECRET.
server/services/supabase.js constructs a privileged Supabase client from SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY.
findings.recommendedFix: Rotate all exposed secrets immediately, remove .env from version control, replace it with .env.example, and audit systems for misuse.

actions.title: Rotate exposed secrets and remove committed .env

findings.title: Moderators are granted broad admin access
findings.issue: requireAdmin() allows moderator, admin, and superadmin, and sensitive /api/admin routes depend on that middleware.
findings.whyItMatters: A lower-privilege role can access cross-tenant data and destructive admin operations.
evidence: server/db/auth.js includes moderator in requireAdmin().
server/api/admin.js uses requireAdmin for /summary, /users, /audit, and tree deletion.
findings.recommendedFix: Split moderator privileges from admin privileges and gate sensitive routes behind admin or superadmin only.

actions.title: Restrict sensitive admin routes to admin and superadmin

findings.title: Service-role storage operations are reachable from user-controlled flows
findings.issue: Upload and delete endpoints accept caller-controlled storage parameters and use a service_role storage client without verifying ownership.
findings.whyItMatters: Any authenticated user can write into another tree namespace or delete files they do not own.
evidence: server/api/uploads.js trusts treeId, personId, category, and url/path from the request.
server/services/file-storage.js uploads and removes objects through supabaseAdmin.
findings.recommendedFix: Derive storage paths on the server from verified ownership, persist file ownership metadata, and authorize deletes against that metadata.

actions.title: Enforce server-side ownership checks for upload and delete paths

findings.title: Any tree member can email the entire tree
findings.issue: The family-update route only checks tree membership before emailing every other member.
findings.whyItMatters: An invited member can spam the whole tree, generate cost, and send misleading messages under the application identity.
evidence: server/api/notifications.js only checks hasAccess(treeId, userId).
The same route sends email to all recipientIds derived from the tree member list.
findings.recommendedFix: Restrict bulk family notifications to tree owner or delegated admins and add route-level rate limits.

actions.title: Limit family-update email actions to authorized tree admins

findings.title: Public signup endpoint creates email-confirmed users and logs credentials
findings.issue: The unauthenticated signup path creates users with email_confirm set true and logs temporary passwords on email failure.
findings.whyItMatters: This bypasses normal verification and risks credential disclosure in logs.
evidence: server/api/auth-secure.js uses supabaseAdmin.auth.admin.createUser with email_confirm: true.
The same route logs temp passwords when email is not configured or fails.
findings.recommendedFix: Remove or heavily restrict this route and use OTP or verified-email signup only. Never log credentials.

actions.title: Retire public temp-password signup and eliminate credential logging

findings.title: Stored XSS through unsanitized innerHTML rendering
findings.issue: User-controlled values like biographies, tree names, descriptions, and discovery messages are rendered via innerHTML without sanitization.
findings.whyItMatters: Malicious content can execute in other users’ browsers, including admin sessions.
evidence: app/js/member-profile.js injects member.biography into HTML.
app/js/app.js injects tree and discovery request fields into HTML templates.
findings.recommendedFix: Escape all untrusted content or render through text nodes. Only allow sanitized rich text where explicitly needed.

actions.title: Sanitize persisted user content before HTML rendering

findings.title: Invite and discovery actions are too broadly available
findings.issue: Any tree member can generate invites, review discovery requests, and invite discovery requesters. The allowMemberInvites tree setting is not enforced.
findings.whyItMatters: Non-owner members can bring outsiders into a tree and access requester PII against product expectations.
evidence: server/api/invites.js only checks tree access before creating invites.
server/api/discovery.js only checks tree access before listing or updating requests.
server/db/schema.sql defines allowMemberInvites in settings but server routes do not enforce it.
findings.recommendedFix: Enforce invite and discovery privileges on the server based on owner/admin role and tree settings.

actions.title: Apply allowMemberInvites and role checks to invite and discovery routes

findings.title: Intended security middleware is not active
findings.issue: The live server disables CSP and does not wire in the project’s stronger security and validation middleware.
findings.whyItMatters: The app misses intended protections, making XSS and input-handling issues easier to exploit.
evidence: server.js calls helmet({ contentSecurityPolicy: false }).
server/middleware/security.js and server/middleware/input-validation.js are present but unused.
findings.recommendedFix: Adopt a single security middleware stack, wire it into server.js, and verify CSP and validation behavior in tests.

actions.title: Enable and validate the intended security middleware stack

findings.title: Admin list endpoints do full-table reads and filter in memory
findings.issue: Admin users and audit endpoints load large result sets into memory and only then filter, paginate, and search.
findings.whyItMatters: This will degrade with data growth and creates avoidable memory pressure and slow responses.
evidence: server/api/admin.js loads all users before filtering.
server/api/admin.js loads all audit rows before filtering and slicing.
findings.recommendedFix: Move search, sort, and pagination into SQL and only return the requested page.

actions.title: Push admin filtering and pagination down into SQL

findings.title: No Supabase RLS or storage policy definitions are present in this repo
findings.issue: The codebase relies on application logic and a service_role backend client but contains no RLS or storage policy migrations.
findings.whyItMatters: There is no database-level defense-in-depth if future code starts querying Supabase directly.
evidence: server/db/schema.sql defines tables but no ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY or CREATE POLICY statements.
server/services/supabase.js creates a service_role Supabase client.
findings.recommendedFix: Add explicit Supabase SQL migrations for RLS and storage policies, even if current access is mostly server-mediated.

actions.title: Create explicit RLS and storage policy migrations for Supabase

findings.title: Critical auth and authorization flows lack automated tests
findings.issue: The automated test surface is dominated by theme/UI tests, with limited coverage of authz, storage, admin permissions, and mutation flows.
findings.whyItMatters: Security regressions are likely to ship unnoticed.
evidence: package.json points test commands at Jest but the visible automated tests are mostly theme-related.
tests/html and tests/auth contain many manual HTML fixtures rather than enforced assertions.
findings.recommendedFix: Add integration tests for role authorization, invite flows, storage ownership, and destructive mutations.

actions.title: Add integration tests for authz, storage ownership, and destructive mutations

missingTests: Authorization matrix tests for member, moderator, admin, and superadmin across sensitive routes.
Upload and delete tests proving users cannot write into or delete another tree namespace.
Notification tests proving non-owners cannot email an entire tree.
XSS regression tests for biographies, tree names, descriptions, and discovery request content.
Invite and discovery tests enforcing allowMemberInvites and owner/admin restrictions.
quickWins: Rotate all committed secrets and remove .env from version control.
Restrict family-update, invite, and admin routes to the minimum required roles.
Add ownership checks to all storage write and delete paths.
Escape or sanitize all user-controlled content before rendering via innerHTML.
longerTermRefactors: Move route-level authorization checks into a shared policy layer.
Create explicit Supabase SQL migrations for RLS, storage policies, and auth sync assumptions.
Replace in-memory admin filtering with SQL pagination and search.
Expand automated integration coverage for auth, authorization, storage, and admin mutations.
